Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, but I made it work. I'm 27 and working part-time, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever pockets of time I could find. I'd usually study in bursts—maybe a couple hours on my days off or late at night after work. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often be doing things physically, like writing out notes or practicing problems, rather than just reading. About 11 hours a week was all I could manage, and I had to be strategic about it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because work was demanding or I was just too anxious to focus. Then when deadlines approached, I'd have these intense study sessions where I'd try to catch up on everything. It wasn't ideal, but with limited resources and competing responsibilities, I had to be flexible. Regular schedules are a luxury I couldn't always afford.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment reminder would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something due, and that would snap me into action. Other times, I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed since my attendance was so low. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me too—seeing those little achievements gave me a small boost when everything else felt overwhelming.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural part of my routine. I used it when I absolutely had to, but it always felt like an extra step. There were brief periods where I tried to make it habitual, like checking in every morning, but my work schedule kept disrupting that. The gamified elements helped a bit—I'd sometimes log in just to see my progress or earn a badge, which was a nice distraction from the stress.
Q5: The hands-on practice activities and any interactive elements were most useful for me. As a kinesthetic learner, I need to actually do things to understand them, not just read or watch. The gamified tasks were surprisingly helpful because they broke things into smaller, manageable pieces that didn't feel as overwhelming. Videos were okay, but I'd usually watch them once and then work through the material myself with pen and paper.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums—I only posted once the whole course. It's not that I didn't value other students' perspectives, but I was so anxious and time-pressed that participating felt like too much. The lengthy reading materials I'd often skim or download and never fully get through. Some resources just seemed redundant, so I'd skip them to focus on what I thought would actually be on the exam.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to passing the course or if it gave me that sense of accomplishment. The gamified tasks were great for this—completing them felt like a small win, which I desperately needed. If I could see how something would help me on an assignment or exam, I'd engage with it. Abstract or theoretical stuff that didn't have clear practical application felt like a waste of my limited time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're juggling work, limited resources, and high anxiety, you have to prioritize ruthlessly. Required activities got done because they had to, even if I was stressed about them. Optional ones, no matter how interesting, usually got pushed aside. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but I had to be realistic about what I could handle.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download materials, print things out when I could afford to, and work through problems by hand. I had notebooks full of practice work that the LMS never saw. I'd also use free resources I found online—YouTube tutorials, open textbooks—because sometimes they explained things better or I could access them without good internet. My actual learning was way more extensive than my online activity showed.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through concepts with just my notes and a pen, completely offline. I'd be at work during breaks, reviewing flashcards I'd made. Or I'd be on the bus, reading printed materials. Sometimes I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay the concepts in my head and practice them on paper for days. The LMS only captured that initial video view, not all the mental work that followed.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my unpredictable schedule and sometimes spotty internet, I learned to download everything I could in one session. I'd watch a video, download the slides, maybe grab the practice problems, and then work through everything offline over the next week. It was more efficient for me and meant I could study during commutes or at work. One download could fuel days of actual studying.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt like I was falling behind in everything. There were weeks where just opening the LMS made my heart race because I knew I'd see all these things I hadn't done. Work picked up extra shifts, and I was exhausted. The combination of high stress, low energy, and feeling overwhelmed made it really hard to engage. I'd sometimes just freeze and avoid everything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me procrastinate because I was scared of doing things wrong. When bills were due and money was tight, studying felt almost impossible because my mind was elsewhere. The LMS became this thing I'd open but not really use during those times.
Q14: Many times. I'd scroll through materials, click on things, maybe start a video, but my mind was completely somewhere else. High anxiety does that—you're physically present but mentally checked out. I'd realize I'd been on the same page for twenty minutes without reading a word. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement time, but I wasn't actually learning anything. It was more like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt too big or when I wasn't sure how to approach them. The anxiety made everything feel more daunting than it probably was. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired or stressed, but tomorrow was usually the same. Important assignments would get pushed until the last possible moment. It wasn't laziness—it was being paralyzed by worry and exhaustion.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to power through, even if it meant staying up late or studying during every break at work. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines that kept me moving. But it also meant my LMS activity was very spiky—nothing for days, then intense bursts.
Q17: Feedback was important but also anxiety-inducing. Good feedback motivated me to keep going and made me feel like my efforts were worth something. Negative or critical feedback, even when constructive, would sometimes make me avoid that area of the course for a while because I'd feel defeated. I appreciated knowing where I stood, but my emotional reaction to feedback definitely affected how quickly I'd engage with the next thing.
Q18: The structure was a double-edged sword. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. But the pace sometimes felt relentless when life got complicated. I appreciated that there were gamified elements that made things feel less heavy. The course having 20 online components meant there was always something to do, which was overwhelming but also meant I could always find something manageable to work on.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they broke things into bite-sized pieces and gave me small rewards that helped with my motivation. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many resources to sort through with my limited time. Also, some activities seemed designed for people who could attend regularly or had more time, which didn't fit my situation. More flexibility would have helped.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my bad days—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but not being able to focus enough to actually complete anything. High anxiety can make you feel like you need to be doing something, so you're active on the platform, but the mental energy to finish work just isn't there. It could also be someone struggling to understand the material and repeatedly reviewing without being able to produce output yet.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My exam score and final grade were passing, but my LMS activity was pretty low. I did a lot of learning offline—working through problems by hand, using external resources, studying during commutes. The LMS doesn't capture all that. Someone might look at my login frequency and think I wasn't trying, but I was working hard in ways that just weren't visible on the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone is anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused for five minutes. They miss the notebooks, the flashcards, the mental rehearsal. They show the skeleton of engagement but not the flesh and blood of actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn it, even when it's hard. It's not just showing up or clicking through things—it's wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement often happened in my head or on paper, not on a screen. It's also about persistence, about coming back even after setbacks. I was engaged with this course; it just didn't always look like it online.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout this course, or how many times I almost gave up but didn't. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the creative ways I learned with limited resources, or the mental energy it took just to keep going. It wouldn't show that despite everything—the stress, the low attendance, the minimal discussion posts—I was fighting hard to pass. And I did pass. That resilience doesn't show up in the data.
